source_metadata.title_raw: Heather L. Stroud - A-18-185 - October 16, 2018 - Carlsbad

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Heather L. Stroud 
Deputy City Attorney 
1200 Carlsbad Village Drive 
Carlsbad, CA 92008 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-185 
Dear Ms. Stroud: 
October 16, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
Government Code section 1090. Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice 
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete 
and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you 
should contact us for additional advice. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097. l(c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Carlsbad from contracting with Aquatic Design 
Group to perform the design services for the Monroe Street Pool facility where Aquatic Design 
performed a needs assessment study for the same facility under a prior contract? 
CONCLUSION 
No. As explained below, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into a design 
services contract with Aquatic Design for the Monroe Street Pool facility by virtue of the prior 
contract to perform a needs assessment study for the same facility. 
FACTS 
You are Deputy City Attorney for the City of Carlsbad seeking advice on behalf of the City 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions under Section 1090. We take verbatim the following 
relevant facts from your letter: 
On April 6, 2016, the City of Carlsbad entered into an agreement 
with Aquatic Design Group, Inc. for a term of one year and total 


File No. A-18-185 
Page No. 2 
compensation not to exceed $16,000 to perform a needs assessment 
study for the Monroe Street Pool. The purpose of the needs assessment 
was to ascertain a scope of work for updating the Monroe Street Pool 
facility. The scope of services consisted of: visiting the project site; 
conducting a comprehensive assessment of the facility's structural, 
mechanical, and electrical systems; invasive testing to identify structural 
conditions (as-needed); and identify any deficiencies under the 
Americans with Disabilities Act in the shower and locker room areas. 
Explicitly excluded from the scope of work were preparation of as built 
drawings; evaluation of buildings, site work, and utilities; destructive 
testing of pool structures; and preparation of design or engineering 
drawings and technical specifications. 
Pursuant to that agreement, Aquatic Design Group, Inc. prepared 
a report dated January 31, 2017, entitled "Needs Assessment Study: 
Monroe Street Pool" which includes a description of the existing 
physical and operational conditions, an inventory of the pool equipment, 
the facility's deficiencies, recommended corrective measures, a list of 
"potential enhancements," and a cost estimate for each item. The City of 
Carlsbad does not intend to pursue all of the recommendations in the 
report. 
The City of Carlsbad is using this report to determine which 
recommendations to implement and to provide as background 
information to potential design and engineering consultants. The firm 
selected to design the facility will need to conduct its own inspection of 
the facility. 
The City of Carlsbad anticipates issuing a request for proposals 
for architectural and engineering design services this fall. The request for 
proposals would include the N~eds Assessment Study as an attachment. 
Aquatic Design Group, Inc. has indicated it is interested in submitting a 
proposal for architectural and engineering design services. 
· 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 


Legal Standard for Independent Contractors Under Section 1090. 
File No. A-18-185 
Page No. 3 
Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court 
recently affirmed the long-standing rule from case law that independent contractors are not 
categorically excluded from Section 1090: "Liability under the statute can extend to independent 
contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v. Superior 
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233.) For example, an independent contractor for a state or 
local government agency that "has a hand in designing and developing the plans and specifications 
for the project," has made or participated in the making of a contract for the construction of the 
project and is therefore prohibited from entering a contract to complete the project. (Davis v. Fresno 
Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-301.) 
However, not all independent contractors are covered by section 1090. (Sahlolbei, supra, at 
p. 240.) "'An individual's status as an official under [section 1090] turns on the extent to which the 
person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the 
public trust."' (Ibid. quoting Hub City Solid Waste Sendees, Inc. v. City of Compton (Hub City) 
(2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1125.) In Hub City, the court held that an independent contractor that 
exerts "considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency" is subject to 
Section 1090. (Hub City, supra, at pp. 1124-1125.) 
Previously, we have advised that consultants who play a limited technical role and are 
removed from influencing the contracting decisions of a public agency may not be subject to 
Section 1090. (La Salle Advice Letter, No. A-17-074 and Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084.) 
Likewise, in the Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A-15-147, we determined that only the primary 
consultant and a "highly involved sub-consultant" were subject to Section 1090. The sub-
consultants who provided technical input, reports, and similar information in a support role to the 
consultants, were not subject to Section 1090. 
Generally, an independent contractor is prohibited from a subsequent project to complete a 
project only if the contractor has had extensive involvement in the initial design and development of 
the project and the nature of the contractor's involvement gives the contractor an unfair advantage 
in the subsequent award of the contract. 
In the Grossman Advice Letter, No. A-17-167(a), we concluded that Section 1090 did not 
apply to prohibit the City of Belmont from entering into a contract with an independent contractor 
that had previously performed services related to that contract for the City. Pursuant to the prior 
contract, the independent contractor performed an assessment and inventory of all the City's Pump 
Stations, which included evaluating the condition of all eleven City Pump Stations, making 
recommendations for improvements, providing cost estimates and submitting an evaluation report 
for each Pump Station. 
Based on those facts, we found that the Section 1090 did not prohibit the City from entering 
a subsequent contract with the same independent contractor to provide design services as well as 
construction and engineering support services (design services contract) for one Pump Station's 
eve.ntual rehabilitation. Specifically, we concluded that the independent contractor did not 
participate in the making of design services contract through its performance of the initial contract. 


FileNo. A-18-185 
Page No. 4 
Moreover, it did not impose considerable influence over the City regarding the design services 
contract, nor did it derive an unfair advantage in procuring that contract by the work it performed 
under the initial contract. 
Aquatic Design Is Not Subject to Section 1090. 
The present matter is similar to Grossman. Under the initial contract, Aquatic Design was 
tasked with performing a needs assessment of the structural, mechanical and electrical systems for 
the pool facility, which included an assessment of pool decking, associated equipment and 
safety/maintenance concerns. In addition, it was responsible for identifying any ADA compliance 
deficiencies associated with the shower/locker area. The resulting report, which is technical in 
nature, identified issues in need of repair, optional potential enhancements to the facility, and a cost 
estimate for planning purposes. 
The facts provided do not suggest that in performing these services under the initial contract, 
Aquatic Design was so extensively involved in the design and development of the Monroe Street 
Pool facility that it obtained an unfair advantage over other potential bidders in the future award of 
the design contract. Importantly, the scope of work itself in the initial contract expressly excluded, 
among other things, preparation of as-built drawings, evaluation of buildings and preparation of 
design or engineering drawings. Moreover, the firm ultimately selected to design the facility will 
need to conduct its own inspection of the facility, and by attaching the report to the request for 
proposals as background information, any potential advantage Aquatic Design might otherwise 
have from preparing the report is arguably diminished. 
Here, as in Grossman, we find that Aquatic Design did not participate in the making of the 
subsequent design services contract through its performance of the initial "assessment needs" 
contract. Moreover, there is no indication that Aquatic Design imposed considerable influence over 
the City regarding the design services contract or derived an unfair advantage as a potential bidder 
for that contract through the work it performed under the initial contract. 
Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into a design services 
contract with Aquatic Designs for the Monroe Street Pool facility. 
JW:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
Assistant General Counsel

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Carlsbad from contracting with Aquatic Design 
Group to perform the design services for the Monroe Street Pool facility where Aquatic Design 
performed a needs assessment study for the same facility under a prior contract?
sections.conclusion:
No. As explained below, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into a design 
services contract with Aquatic Design for the Monroe Street Pool facility by virtue of the prior 
contract to perform a needs assessment study for the same facility.

CONCLUSION: No. As explained below, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into a design 
services contract with Aquatic Design for the Monroe Street Pool facility by virtue of the prior 
contract to perform a needs assessment study for the same facility.
